document.title: Usability Validation Report for HnX-P1, HnX-PB Portable X-ray System
document.revisionOrVersion: Report No.: A-UVR-HNX (Rev.1); revision history lists Rev. 2
standards: FDA Guidance - Applying Human Factors and Usability Engineering to Medical Devices

sourceSummary.oneLine: Usability validation report for the HnX-P1 and HnX-PB portable X-ray system covering intended use, usability standards, validation activities, task results, and conclusions.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Supports usability validation evidence for the Portable X-ray System models HnX-P1 and HnX-PB, including safety-critical tasks, user interface evaluation, and residual usability risk conclusions.
keyPoints: The HnX-P1 and HnX-PB are described as battery-powered portable X-ray systems intended for generating radiographic images of human anatomy.
The HnX-PB variant is stated to have only exterior design changes compared with HnX-P1, with interface, material, internal structure, PCB, and software unchanged.
Applicable standards and guidelines listed include EN 60601-1-6:2010, IEC 62366-1:2015, FDA human factors guidance, and ISO 14971.
Validation activities used 15 radiology technician participants in hospital environments.
All listed usability tasks report 100% completion and pass results.
The conclusion states residual risk regarding usability is minimized and acceptable, with re-evaluation to occur once new use-error issues are raised.

concepts.name: Intended use
concepts.evidence: The HnX-P1 (and HnX-PB) is a battery-powered portable X-ray system intended for generating radiographic images of human anatomy.

concepts.name: Usability validation
concepts.evidence: This report applies to all usability validation activities for the Portable X-ray System, Model HnX-P1, focusing on safety-critical tasks, user interface evaluation, and error minimization.

concepts.name: Summative evaluation
concepts.evidence: The summative evaluation is an integral part of the validation process, providing final confirmation that the device meets usability requirements

concepts.name: Residual usability risk
concepts.evidence: Usability verification & evaluation results and improvement action demonstrated that residual risk regarding usability is minimized and acceptable.

evidence.claim: The document is a usability validation report for HnX-P1 and HnX-PB Portable X-ray System.
evidence.evidenceText: Usability Validation Report for HnX-P1, HnX-PB Portable X-ray System
evidence.pageTarget: Usability Validation Report - HnX-P1 and HnX-PB

evidence.claim: The document identifies March 03, 2026 as preparation, review, approval, and Rev. 2 revision date.
evidence.evidenceText: Prepared R&D team Jeong, Yeji March 03, 2026; Review R&D team Han, jimin March 03, 2026; Approval Quality Manager Hur Namkyu March 03, 2026; 2 March 03, 2026 Revision according to the intended purpose
evidence.pageTarget: Usability Validation Report - HnX-P1 and HnX-PB

evidence.claim: The report number shown on the cover is A-UVR-HNX (Rev.1).
The revision history lists Rev. 0 establishment, Rev. 1 addition of HnX-PB variant model, and Rev. 2 revision according to intended purpose.
evidence.evidenceText: 0 Dec 30, 2024 Establishment; 1 May 04, 2025 Add variant model (HnX-PB); 2 March 03, 2026 Revision according to the intended purpose

evidence.claim: HnX-P1 and HnX-PB are intended to generate radiographic images of human anatomy.
evidence.evidenceText: The HnX-P1 (and HnX-PB) is a battery-powered portable X-ray system intended for generating radiographic images of human anatomy.

evidence.claim: HnX-PB is a variant model with only exterior design changes compared with HnX-P1.
evidence.evidenceText: The variant model(HnX-PB) has only changed the exterior design compared to the existing model HnX-P1, and the interface, exterior material, internal structure, PCB, software, etc. are all the same.

evidence.claim: No additional usability validation was planned for adding HnX-PB.
evidence.evidenceText: Therefore, no additional usability validation will be conducted for the addition of the variant model(HnX-PB).

evidence.claim: Applicable standards and guidelines include EN 60601-1-6:2010, IEC 62366-1:2015, FDA human factors guidance, and ISO 14971.
evidence.evidenceText: EN 60601-1-6:2010; IEC 62366-1:2015; FDA Guidance - Applying Human Factors and Usability Engineering to Medical Devices; ISO 14971 - Medical Devices Application of Risk Management to Medical Devices

evidence.claim: Validation activities evaluated usability in simulated real-world environments and included formative and summative evaluations.
evidence.evidenceText: Validation activities were conducted to evaluate the usability of the HnX-P1 in simulated real-world environments. Both formative and summative evaluations were carried out to assess the product's safety, effectiveness, and user satisfaction.

evidence.claim: The validation objectives were safe and effective use by intended users and mitigation of residual usability risks.
evidence.evidenceText: Verify that the product can be used safely and effectively by intended users. Identify and mitigate any residual usability risks.

evidence.claim: The summative evaluation outcome states no critical usability issues were identified.
evidence.evidenceText: Outcomes: No critical usability issues were identified.

evidence.claim: The usability validation involved 15 participants with demographics listed as radiology technicians.
evidence.evidenceText: Number of Participants: 15 Demographics: Radiology technicians

evidence.claim: The test environments included Hope Hospital (Byeongjeom) and Samyuk Seoul Hospital.
evidence.evidenceText: Location 장소 Hope Hospital (Byeongjeom) 희망찬병원(병점) Samyuk Seoul Hospital 삼육서울병원

evidence.claim: Each usability task reported a 100% pass result.
evidence.evidenceText: Task Completion Success Rate Criteria: A total completion rate of 100 % in each usability task. Result: 100 % (Pass)

evidence.claim: The overall data summary reports ACT #1 through ACT #11 as pass.
evidence.evidenceText: Usability Test Act Task Completion ACT #1 ACT #2 ACT #3 ACT #4 ACT #5 ACT #6 ACT #7 ACT #8 ACT #9 ACT #10 ACT #11 Pass Pass Pass Pass Pass Pass Pass Pass Pass Pass Pass

evidence.claim: The conclusion states residual usability risk is minimized and acceptable.
evidence.evidenceText: Usability verification & evaluation results and improvement action demonstrated that residual risk regarding usability is minimized and acceptable.

evidence.claim: The conclusion states no benefit-outweigh-risk evaluation was performed because the usability goal met acceptance criteria.
evidence.evidenceText: Since the usability verification & evaluation report demonstrates that the usability goal met the acceptance criteria set in the usability verification & evaluation plan, no evaluation of “benefit outweigh risk” is performed.

evidence.claim: Re-evaluation of usability is to be performed once new use-error issues are raised.
evidence.evidenceText: The re-evaluation of usability will be performed once new use-error issues are raised.

evidence.claim: Attached documents are A-USEP-HNX Usability Summative Evaluation Plan and A-USER-HNX Usability Summative Evaluation Report.
evidence.evidenceText: Attached documents A-USEP-HNX Usability Summative Evaluation Plan A-USER-HNX Usability Summative Evaluation Report

reviewFlags.reason: Revision metadata is conflicting: cover text states Report No.: A-UVR-HNX (Rev.1), while the revision history includes Rev. 2 dated March 03, 2026 and the source path names Rev.2.
Authority is ambiguous: source metadata signals FDA and the text lists FDA Guidance, but the document does not state an FDA submission, clearance, approval, or jurisdictional decision.
Scope is inconsistent: the report title and purpose include HnX-P1 and HnX-PB, but the Scope section says the report applies to Model HnX-P1.
Some acceptance criteria use impossible wording such as 'over 100% of users', suggesting document wording or OCR quality issues.

Further Improvement states some problems were carried out during usability testing, while the summative evaluation outcome states no critical usability issues were identified; the severity and details of the problems are not specified.
